name: Colorado SB 24-205 (AI Consumer Protections)
description: Colorado state law regulating high-risk AI systems making consequential decisions affecting consumers. Effective February 1, 2026.
verification_note: MATERIAL FINDING: SB 24-205 never took effect. Its effective date slipped twice (originally 2026-02-01; postponed to 2026-06-30 by SB 25B-004, signed 2025-08-28) and it was then repealed outright before reaching that delayed date -- effective_from is left null because it never bound anyone; effective_to is set to 2026-05-14, the date Governor Polis signed the repeal (SB 26-189), since that is when this statute's independent force ended (see docs/framework_authoring_guide.md section 7's 'repealed before ever effective' case). SB 26-189 repeals and replaces this framework with a narrower disclosure-and-rights model for automated decision-making technology (ADMT) -- modeled Week 3 as the new framework_id colorado_admt (superseded_by, above), since the statutory identity, defined terms, and substantive obligations all changed (no duty of care against algorithmic discrimination, no risk-management program, no impact assessment survive). Do not cite this dataset's tiers/controls to a client as current or forthcoming Colorado law -- see colorado_admt.json for what actually applies once it takes effect, and its verification_note for an unresolved effective-date discrepancy in the sources found so far. Verified via web search 2026-07-09 (Week 2) and 2026-07-10 (Week 3 parallel cleanup). Sources: Akin Gump, 'Colorado Postpones Implementation of Colorado AI Act, SB 24-205' (https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/ai-law-and-regulation-tracker/colorado-postpones-implementation-of-colorado-ai-act-sb-24-205); Norton Rose Fulbright, 'Colorado enacts revised AI law' (https://www.nortonrosefulbright.com/en-us/knowledge/publications/18733d31/colorado-enacts-revised-ai-law); McDermott Will & Emery, 'Colorado AI law in flux' (https://www.mcdermottlaw.com/insights/colorado-ai-law-in-flux-comprehensive-replacement-bill-signed-after-federal-court-blocks-predecessors-enforcement/); Seyfarth Shaw, 'Colorado Enacts Artificial Intelligence Replacement Law' (https://www.seyfarth.com/news-insights/colorado-enacts-artificial-intelligence-replacement-law.html); Colorado General Assembly, SB26-189 bill page (https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189).

criteria.summary: AI systems that make, or are a substantial factor in making, a consequential decision affecting consumers.

consequential_decision_categories.employment: Employment or employment opportunity (hiring, firing, promotion, terms of compensation)
consequential_decision_categories.financial_lending: Financial or lending services (loan approval, credit, interest rate determination)

criteria.substantial_factor_definition: An AI system is a 'substantial factor' if it: (a) assists in making a consequential decision; (b) is capable of altering the outcome of the decision; (c) is used to generate any content, decision, prediction, or recommendation related to the consumer.

criteria.summary: AI systems not making consequential decisions, or that fall under specific exemptions.
exemptions: AI systems used solely for research
Systems compliant with federal law providing equivalent protection
